content:
# Matthew Chapter 14 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 14 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 14:1 -- "fame" vs. "report" vs. "reports"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "heard the report of the Yeshua" | Literal akoe (hearing/report) |
| LOGOS | "heard about the fame of Jesus" | Interprets akoe as "fame" |
| PNEUMA | "heard the reports about Jesus" | Dynamic plural |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "fame." The Greek akoe can mean "hearing, report, fame." In context, Herod hears about Jesus' widespread reputation, making "fame" appropriate. EMET's "report" is neutral; PNEUMA's plural "reports" is reasonable but the Greek is singular.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:2 -- "miraculous powers" vs. "powers" vs. "miracles"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the powers work in him" | Literal dynameis |
| LOGOS | "these miraculous powers are at work in him" | Formal with "miraculous" added |
| PNEUMA | "That's why he can do these miracles" | Dynamic paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "miraculous powers." The Greek dynameis means "powers/mighty works." Adding "miraculous" clarifies for English readers. PNEUMA's restructuring as "he can do these miracles" changes the agency -- the Greek says the powers are at work in him, not that he does miracles.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:9 -- "grieved" vs. "sorry" vs. "distressed"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the king was grieved" | Literal lupetheis |
| LOGOS | "the king was sorry" | Softer rendering |
| PNEUMA | "The king was distressed" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "grieved" (EMET), modifying LOGOS's "sorry" which understates the Greek lupeo (to grieve, be deeply troubled). "Distressed" (PNEUMA) is also adequate. SOPHIA chose "grieved" as it better captures the emotional weight appropriate to the gravity of the decision.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET. "Grieved" is more fitting than "sorry" for a king ordering an execution.

---

## 14:25 -- "fourth watch of the night" vs. "Between three and six in the morning"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the fourth watch of the night" | Literal with Roman time-keeping term |
| LOGOS | "in the fourth watch of the night" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "Between three and six in the morning" | Converts to modern time |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's "fourth watch of the night." This preserves the ancient time-keeping reference and its narrative connotation (the darkest, most exhausted hour). PNEUMA's modern conversion is helpful as a note but removes the cultural texture from the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 14:26 -- "ghost" vs. "phantom"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "It is a phantom!" | Literal phantasma |
| LOGOS | "It is a ghost!" | Common English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "It's a ghost!" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA's "ghost." While "phantom" (EMET, from phantasma) is etymologically precise, "ghost" is the natural English word for an apparition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 14:27 -- "Take heart; it is I" vs. "Take courage; I am" vs. "Courage! It's me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Take courage; I am; do not fear" | Preserves ego eimi as "I am" (divine name echo) |
| LOGOS | "Take heart; it is I. Do not be afraid" | Formal with "it is I" |
| PNEUMA | "Courage! It's me. Don't be afraid" | Colloquial rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "Take heart; it is I. Do not be afraid." This preserves the formal register of a theophanic moment while remaining natural English. EMET's "I am" preserves the divine-name echo but reads awkwardly as dialogue. PNEUMA's "It's me" is too colloquial for a scene laden with OT theophanic significance (walking on water, divine self-identification).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The ego eimi resonance is noted in translation notes rather than forced into the dialogue.

---

## 14:19 -- "said a blessing" vs. "blessed" vs. "gave thanks"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he blessed" | Literal eulogeo |
| LOGOS | "said a blessing" | Formal equivalent expanding "blessed" |
| PNEUMA | "gave thanks" | Dynamic rendering -- interprets blessing as thanksgiving |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "said a blessing." The Greek eulogesen means "he blessed" or "he spoke a blessing." PNEUMA's "gave thanks" anticipates the language of the second feeding (15:36, eucharistesas) and the Last Supper. In this passage, the word is eulogeo (to bless), not eucharisteo (to give thanks), and the distinction should be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The eulogeo/eucharisteo distinction is maintained.

---

## 14:24 -- "many stadia from the land" vs. "far from shore"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "many stadia from the land" | Preserves Greek unit of measurement |
| LOGOS | "many stadia from the land" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "far from shore" | Dynamic simplification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's "many stadia from the land." Stadion is a known ancient measurement (~600 feet), and preserving it maintains the text's historical texture. A note can provide the conversion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 14:33 -- "worshiped him" vs. "fell down before him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "worshiped him" | Literal proskuneo |
| LOGOS | "worshiped him" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "fell down before him" | Renders proskuneo as physical action |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's "worshiped." The verb proskuneo means to prostrate oneself in worship. In this context -- following a theophanic event (walking on water, ego eimi) and a confession ("Truly you are the Son of God") -- "worshiped" is the correct rendering. PNEUMA's "fell down before him" describes only the physical gesture, not the worship.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 14:36 -- "fringe of his garment" vs. "edge of his robe"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the fringe of the garment of him" | Literal kraspedou with possessive |
| LOGOS | "the fringe of his garment" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "the edge of his robe" | Dynamic simplification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "fringe of his garment." The Greek kraspedon refers specifically to the tassels or fringes (tzitzit) on a Jewish garment (Numbers 15:38-39). "Edge" (PNEUMA) loses this specific reference to the ritual fringes.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The tzitzit reference is preserved.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase vs. LOGOS/EMET | 8 | LOGOS/EMET preferred |
| EMET agrees with LOGOS vs. PNEUMA | 5 | EMET/LOGOS adopted |
| SOPHIA sides with EMET over LOGOS | 1 | "Grieved" over "sorry" (14:9) |
| Cultural/historical terms preserved | 3 | Fourth watch, stadia, fringe retained |
| Theophanic register maintained | 2 | "It is I" and "worshiped" preserved |

### Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 14 is primarily narrative, and disagreements are fewer and less theologically charged than in the discourse chapters. The most significant divergences involve the theophanic sea-walking episode (vv.25-33), where PNEUMA's colloquial register ("It's me," "fell down before him") flattens the OT divine-encounter echoes that all three translators recognize in their notes. SOPHIA's decision to prefer "grieved" over LOGOS's "sorry" in v.9 is noteworthy as a rare departure from LOGOS, driven by the gravity of the narrative moment. The eulogeo/eucharisteo distinction in v.19 represents an important precision preserved in the consensus text.

---

*Log compiled by SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
